content: DVT diagnosis: clinical pretest probability using Wells score (clinical signs/symptoms, active cancer, paralysis/immobilization, bedridden >3 days, localized tenderness, entire leg swelling, calf swelling >3cm vs asymptomatic side, pitting edema, collateral superficial veins, alternative diagnosis less likely than DVT). Score ≥2: likely DVT; <2: unlikely. Unlikely DVT: D-dimer first (high sensitivity ~95%, low specificity); if negative, DVT ruled out; if positive, proceed to ultrasound. Likely DVT or positive D-dimer: compression ultrasonography—noncompressible vein diagnostic. Single proximal ultrasound sufficient if D-dimer negative or low clinical suspicion; if high suspicion with negative initial ultrasound, repeat in 1 week (to detect propagating calf DVT) or perform whole-leg ultrasound. MRI/MRV for pelvic vein thrombosis or contraindications to ultrasound. Contrast venography gold standard but rarely used (invasive, nephrotoxic).
